mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm replaces the page that has been in memory the longest time?

options.text: LRU (Least Recently Used)
FIFO (First In First Out)
Optimal Page Replacement
LFU (Least Frequently Used)

mcqs.explanation: FIFO maintains a queue of pages in memory order, removing the oldest page (first in) when a page fault occurs.

mcqs.question: Consider a system with 3 frames. Reference string: 4, 3, 2, 1, 4, 3, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 5. Using FIFO, how many page faults occur?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO produces page faults for: 4,3,2,1,4 (fault),3 (fault),5 (fault replaces 4),4 (fault replaces 2),3,2 (fault replaces 1),1 (fault replaces 5),5 (fault replaces 4) = 11 faults.

mcqs.question: Belady's Anomaly demonstrates that:

options.text: Increasing frames always decreases page faults
Some page replacement algorithms can suffer more page faults when frames are increased
Page replacement algorithms are independent of frame count
LRU always outperforms FIFO

mcqs.explanation: Belady's Anomaly shows that FIFO can have MORE page faults when more physical memory frames are added, unlike stack algorithms like LRU.

mcqs.question: In LRU page replacement, which page is evicted when a page fault occurs?

options.text: The page with the lowest usage count
The page that has been referenced most recently
The page that has not been used for the longest time
The page that was loaded first

mcqs.explanation: LRU evicts the page that has not been used for the longest period of time, approximating the principle of temporal locality.

mcqs.question: Given 4 frames and reference string: A,B,C,D,E,A,B,F,E,A,B,C,D,F, calculate page faults using LRU. How many page faults occur?
mcqs.explanation: LRU with 4 frames: A,B,C,D (4 faults), E (fault, replace A), A (fault, replace B), B, F (fault, replace C), E (fault, replace D), A (fault, replace F), B, C (fault, replace E), D (fault, replace A), F (fault, replace B) = 14 faults.

mcqs.question: The Optimal page replacement algorithm is impractical because:

options.text: It requires too much hardware support
It cannot be implemented in real systems
It requires future knowledge of page references which is impossible
It has high computational overhead

mcqs.explanation: Optimal replacement evicts the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future, but this requires knowing the entire reference string in advance, which is not possible in practice.

mcqs.question: In LFU (Least Frequently Used) replacement, when two pages have equal frequency counts, which tie-breaking strategy is typically used?
mcqs.text: Remove the page with the oldest timestamp
Remove the page with the most recent reference
Remove the page with the smallest frame number
Randomly select one of the pages

options.text: Remove the page with the oldest timestamp
Remove the page with the most recent reference
Remove the page with the smallest frame number
Randomly select one of the pages

mcqs.explanation: When page counts are equal in LFU, the page with the oldest arrival time (oldest timestamp) is typically evicted as a tiebreaker.

mcqs.question: A system using LRU replacement experiences thrashing. Which of the following is the most appropriate solution?

options.text: Increase the degree of multiprogramming
Reduce the number of frames allocated to processes
Increase the number of frames allocated to processes
Switch to FIFO replacement

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when processes don't have enough frames, causing excessive page faults. Increasing allocated frames gives each process more memory to work with, reducing page faults.
